reasoning: The current Overnight Bank Funding Rate (OBFR) is structurally anchored at 4.33, guided by the FOMC's target range. For 2025-09-07, there is no scheduled FOMC meeting, so the target range will remain unchanged. Given the robust liquidity buffers (like the Standing Repo Facility) the Fed uses to maintain the rate, the chance of an unexpected technical spike exceeding 4.33 is near zero. For 2025-09-30 and 2025-11-29, the market (via CME FedWatch) is pricing in an ~90% probability of a 25 bps rate cut at the September 16-17 FOMC meeting, and expects further cuts in November. Therefore, for OBFR to be >4.33 on these dates, either the Fed would have to deliver a historically surprising rate hike, or a massive funding market crisis would have to drive rates drastically above the target range, both of which are extremely unlikely. For 2026-02-27, while the base case remains a continued easing cycle, the longer 6-month horizon introduces a marginal possibility of an inflation resurgence or shift in policy under a new administration, slightly raising the tail risk of a rate hike. Consequently, the probability of an increase starts at the system minimum and rises marginally to reflect increasing time horizon uncertainty.
